**Early**: Nausea, vomiting, skin burns, loss of appetite[[1]](#cite_note-CDC2019-1) **Later**: Infections, bleeding, dehydration, confusion[[1]](#cite_note-CDC2019-1)
Bone marrow syndrome, gastrointestinal syndrome, neurovascular syndrome[[1]](#cite_note-CDC2019-1)[[3]](#cite_note-SMJ2010-3)

Nausea, vomiting, skin burns, loss of appetite[[1]](#cite_note-CDC2019-1) **Later**: Infections, bleeding, dehydration, confusion[[1]](#cite_note-CDC2019-1) |

(**ARS**), also known as **radiation sickness** or **radiation poisoning**, is a collection of health effects that are caused by being exposed to high [amounts](/wiki/Absorbed_dose "Absorbed dose") of [ionizing radiation](/wiki/Ionizing_radiation "Ionizing radiation") in a short period of time.[[1]](#cite_note-CDC2019-1) Symptoms can start within an hour of exposure, and can last for several months.[[1]](#cite_note-CDC2019-1)[[3]](#cite_note-SMJ2010-3)[[5]](#cite_note-Review09-5) Early symptoms are usually nausea, vomiting and loss of appetite.[[1]](#cite_note-CDC2019-1) In the following hours or weeks, initial symptoms may appear to improve, before the development of additional symptoms, after which either recovery or death follows.[[1]](#cite_note-CDC2019-1)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Radiation poisoning](/w/index.php?title=Radiation_poisoning&redirect=no "Radiation poisoning"))

Result of nuclear radiation exposure

"Radiation poisoning" redirects here. For other uses, see [Radiation poisoning (disambiguation)](/wiki/Radiation_poisoning_(disambiguation) "Radiation poisoning (disambiguation)").

Medical condition

**Acute radiation syndrome** (**ARS**), also known as **radiation sickness** or **radiation poisoning**, is a collection of health effects that are caused by being exposed to high [amounts](/wiki/Absorbed_dose "Absorbed dose") of [ionizing radiation](/wiki/Ionizing_radiation "Ionizing radiation") in a short period of time.[[1]](#cite_note-CDC2019-1) Symptoms can start within an hour of exposure, and can last for several months.[[1]](#cite_note-CDC2019-1)[[3]](#cite_note-SMJ2010-3)[[5]](#cite_note-Review09-5) Early symptoms are usually nausea, vomiting and loss of appetite.[[1]](#cite_note-CDC2019-1) In the following hours or weeks, initial symptoms may appear to improve, before the development of additional symptoms, after which either recovery or death follows.[[1]](#cite_note-CDC2019-1)

ARS involves a total dose of greater than 0.7 [Gy](/wiki/Gray_(unit) "Gray (unit)") (70 [rad](/wiki/Rad_(unit) "Rad (unit)")), that generally occurs from a source outside the body, delivered within a few minutes.[[1]](#cite_note-CDC2019-1) Sources of such radiation can occur accidentally or intentionally.[[6]](#cite_note-6) They may involve [nuclear reactors](/wiki/Nuclear_reactors "Nuclear reactors"), [cyclotrons](/wiki/Cyclotrons "Cyclotrons"), certain devices used in [cancer therapy](/wiki/Cancer_therapy "Cancer therapy"), [nuclear weapons](/wiki/Nuclear_weapons "Nuclear weapons"), or [radiological weapons](/wiki/Radiological_weapons "Radiological weapons").[[4]](#cite_note-NORD2019-4) It is generally divided into three types: bone marrow, gastrointestinal, and neurovascular syndrome, with bone marrow syndrome occurring at 0.7 to 10 Gy, and neurovascular syndrome occurring at doses that exceed 50 Gy.[[1]](#cite_note-CDC2019-1)[[3]](#cite_note-SMJ2010-3) The [cells](/wiki/Cell_(biology) "Cell (biology)") that are most affected are generally those that are rapidly dividing.[[3]](#cite_note-SMJ2010-3) At high doses, this causes DNA damage that may be irreparable.[[4]](#cite_note-NORD2019-4) Diagnosis is based on a history of exposure and symptoms.[[4]](#cite_note-NORD2019-4) Repeated [complete blood counts](/wiki/Complete_blood_count "Complete blood count") (CBCs) can indicate the severity of exposure.[[1]](#cite_note-CDC2019-1)

Treatment of ARS is generally [supportive care](/wiki/Supportive_care "Supportive care"). This may include [blood transfusions](/wiki/Blood_transfusions "Blood transfusions"), [antibiotics](/wiki/Antibiotics "Antibiotics"), [colony-stimulating factors](/wiki/Colony-stimulating_factors "Colony-stimulating factors"), or [stem cell transplant](/wiki/Stem_cell_transplant "Stem cell transplant").[[3]](#cite_note-SMJ2010-3) Radioactive material remaining on the skin or in the stomach should be removed. If [radioiodine](/wiki/Iodine-131 "Iodine-131") was inhaled or ingested, [potassium iodide](/wiki/Potassium_iodide "Potassium iodide") is recommended. Complications such as [leukemia](/wiki/Leukemia "Leukemia") and other [cancers](/wiki/Cancers "Cancers") among those who survive are managed as usual. Short-term outcomes depend on the dose exposure.[[4]](#cite_note-NORD2019-4)

ARS is generally rare.[[3]](#cite_note-SMJ2010-3) A single event can affect a large number of people.[[7]](#cite_note-7) The vast majority of cases involving ARS, alongside [blast effects](/wiki/Effects_of_nuclear_explosions "Effects of nuclear explosions"), were inflicted by the [atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki](/wiki/Atomic_bombings_of_Hiroshima_and_Nagasaki "Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki"), with post-attack deaths in the tens of thousands. [Nuclear and radiation accidents and incidents](/wiki/Nuclear_and_radiation_accidents_and_incidents "Nuclear and radiation accidents and incidents") sometimes cause ARS; the worst, the [Chernobyl nuclear power plant disaster](/wiki/Chernobyl_disaster "Chernobyl disaster"), caused 134 cases and 28 deaths.[[1]](#cite_note-CDC2019-1) ARS differs from [chronic radiation syndrome](/wiki/Chronic_radiation_syndrome "Chronic radiation syndrome"), which occurs following prolonged exposures to relatively low doses of radiation, and from [radiation-induced cancer](/wiki/Radiation-induced_cancer "Radiation-induced cancer").[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## Signs and symptoms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_radiation_syndrome&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Signs and symptoms")]

See also: [Effects of nuclear explosions on human health](/wiki/Effects_of_nuclear_explosions_on_human_health "Effects of nuclear explosions on human health")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Radiation_Sickness.png/500px-Radiation_Sickness.png)](/wiki/File:Radiation_Sickness.png)

Radiation sickness

Classically, ARS is divided into three main presentations: [hematopoietic](/wiki/Haematopoiesis "Haematopoiesis"), [gastrointestinal](/wiki/Gastrointestinal "Gastrointestinal"), and [neuro](/wiki/Neurological "Neurological")[vascular](/wiki/Blood_vessel "Blood vessel"). These syndromes may be preceded by a [prodrome](/wiki/Prodrome "Prodrome").[[3]](#cite_note-SMJ2010-3) The speed of symptom onset is related to radiation exposure, with greater doses resulting in a shorter delay in symptom onset.[[3]](#cite_note-SMJ2010-3) These presentations presume whole-body exposure, and many of them are markers that are invalid if the entire body has not been exposed. Each syndrome requires that the tissue showing the syndrome itself be exposed (e.g., gastrointestinal syndrome is not seen if the stomach and intestines are not exposed to radiation). Some areas affected are:

1. Hematopoietic. This syndrome is marked by a drop in the number of [blood cells](/wiki/Blood_cells "Blood cells"), called [aplastic anemia](/wiki/Aplastic_anemia "Aplastic anemia"). This may result in infections, due to a low number of [white blood cells](/wiki/White_blood_cells "White blood cells"), bleeding, due to a lack of [platelets](/wiki/Platelets "Platelets"), and [anemia](/wiki/Anemia "Anemia"), due to too few [red blood cells](/wiki/Red_blood_cells "Red blood cells") in circulation.[[3]](#cite_note-SMJ2010-3) These changes can be detected by blood tests after receiving a whole-body acute dose as low as 0.25 [grays](/wiki/Gray_(unit) "Gray (unit)") (25 [rad](/wiki/Rad_(unit) "Rad (unit)")), though they might never be felt by the patient if the dose is below 1 gray (100 rad). Conventional trauma and burns resulting from a bomb blast are complicated by the poor wound healing caused by hematopoietic syndrome, increasing mortality.
2. Gastrointestinal. This syndrome often follows absorbed doses of 6–30 grays (600–3,000 rad).[[3]](#cite_note-SMJ2010-3) The signs and symptoms of this form of radiation injury include [nausea](/wiki/Nausea "Nausea"), [vomiting](/wiki/Vomiting "Vomiting"), [loss of appetite](/wiki/Anorexia_(symptom) "Anorexia (symptom)"), and [abdominal pain](/wiki/Abdominal_pain "Abdominal pain").[[10]](#cite_note-Christensen2014-10) Vomiting in this time-frame (1-2h post-exposure) is a marker for whole body exposures that are in the fatal range above 4 grays (400 rad). Without exotic treatment such as [bone marrow transplant](/wiki/Bone_marrow_transplant "Bone marrow transplant"), death with this dose is common,[[3]](#cite_note-SMJ2010-3) due generally more to infection than gastrointestinal dysfunction.
3. Neurovascular. This syndrome typically occurs at absorbed doses greater than 30 grays (3,000 rad), though it may occur at doses as low as 10 grays (1,000 rad).[[3]](#cite_note-SMJ2010-3) It presents with neurological symptoms such as [dizziness](/wiki/Dizziness "Dizziness"), [headache](/wiki/Headache "Headache"), or [decreased level of consciousness](/wiki/Decreased_level_of_consciousness "Decreased level of consciousness"), occurring within minutes to a few hours, with an absence of vomiting, and is almost always fatal, even with aggressive intensive care.[[3]](#cite_note-SMJ2010-3)

Early symptoms of ARS typically include nausea, vomiting, headaches, fatigue, [fever](/wiki/Fever "Fever"), and a short period of [skin reddening](/wiki/Erythema "Erythema").[[3]](#cite_note-SMJ2010-3) These symptoms may occur at radiation doses as low as 0.35 grays (35 rad). These symptoms are common to many illnesses, and may not, by themselves, indicate acute radiation sickness.[[3]](#cite_note-SMJ2010-3)

### Skin changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_radiation_syndrome&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Skin changes")]

Main article: [Radiation burn](/wiki/Radiation_burn "Radiation burn")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Daghlian-hand.jpg/250px-Daghlian-hand.jpg)](/wiki/File:Daghlian-hand.jpg)

[Harry K. Daghlian](/wiki/Harry_K._Daghlian "Harry K. Daghlian")'s hand 9 days after he had manually stopped a [prompt critical](/wiki/Prompt_criticality "Prompt criticality") fission reaction during an accident with what later obtained the nickname the [demon core](/wiki/Demon_core "Demon core"). He received a dose of 5.1 [Sv](/wiki/Sievert "Sievert"),[[18]](#cite_note-18) or 3.1 [Gy](/wiki/Gray_(unit) "Gray (unit)").[[19]](#cite_note-hempelman-19) He died 16 days after this photo was taken.

[Cutaneous radiation syndrome](/wiki/List_of_cutaneous_conditions#Ionizing_radiation-induced "List of cutaneous conditions") (CRS) refers to the skin symptoms of radiation exposure.[[1]](#cite_note-CDC2019-1) Within a few hours after irradiation, a transient and inconsistent [redness](/wiki/Erythema "Erythema") (associated with [itching](/wiki/Itch "Itch")) can occur. Then, a latent phase may occur and last from a few days up to several weeks, when intense reddening, [blistering](/wiki/Blister "Blister"), and [ulceration](/wiki/Ulcer_(dermatology) "Ulcer (dermatology)") of the irradiated site is visible. In most cases, healing occurs by regenerative means; however, very large skin doses can cause permanent hair loss, damaged [sebaceous](/wiki/Sebaceous_gland "Sebaceous gland") and [sweat glands](/wiki/Sweat_gland "Sweat gland"), [atrophy](/wiki/Atrophy "Atrophy"), [fibrosis](/wiki/Fibrosis "Fibrosis") (mostly [keloids](/wiki/Keloids "Keloids")), decreased or increased skin pigmentation, and ulceration or [necrosis](/wiki/Necrosis "Necrosis") of the exposed tissue.[[1]](#cite_note-CDC2019-1)

As seen at [Chernobyl](/wiki/Chernobyl_disaster "Chernobyl disaster"), when skin is irradiated with high energy [beta particles](/wiki/Beta_particles "Beta particles"), moist [desquamation](/wiki/Desquamation "Desquamation") (peeling of skin) and similar early effects can heal, only to be followed by the collapse of the dermal vascular system after two months, resulting in the loss of the full thickness of the exposed skin.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Another example of skin loss caused by high-level exposure of radiation is during the [1999 Tokaimura nuclear accident](/wiki/Tokaimura_nuclear_accidents#1999_accident "Tokaimura nuclear accidents"), where technician Hisashi Ouchi had lost a majority of his skin due to the high amounts of radiation he absorbed during the irradiation. This effect had been demonstrated previously with pig skin using high energy beta sources at the Churchill Hospital Research Institute, in [Oxford](/wiki/Oxford "Oxford").[[21]](#cite_note-21)

## Cause

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_radiation_syndrome&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Cause")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fb/Death_by_haematopoietic_syndrome_of_radiation_sickness-_influence_of_dose_rate.png/500px-Death_by_haematopoietic_syndrome_of_radiation_sickness-_influence_of_dose_rate.png)](/wiki/File:Death_by_haematopoietic_syndrome_of_radiation_sickness-_influence_of_dose_rate.png)

Both dose and dose rate contribute to the severity of acute radiation syndrome. The effects of [dose fractionation](/wiki/Dose_fractionation "Dose fractionation") or rest periods before repeated exposure also shift the [LD50](/wiki/LD50 "LD50") dose upwards.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/PIA17601-Comparisons-RadiationExposure-MarsTrip-20131209.png/500px-PIA17601-Comparisons-RadiationExposure-MarsTrip-20131209.png)](/wiki/File:PIA17601-Comparisons-RadiationExposure-MarsTrip-20131209.png)

Comparison of Radiation Doses – includes the amount detected on the trip from Earth to Mars by the [RAD](/wiki/Radiation_assessment_detector "Radiation assessment detector") on the [MSL](/wiki/Mars_Science_Laboratory "Mars Science Laboratory") (2011–2013).[[22]](#cite_note-SCI-20130531a-22)[[23]](#cite_note-SCI-20130531b-23)[[24]](#cite_note-NYT-20130530-24)[[25]](#cite_note-SN-20130629-25)

ARS is caused by exposure to a large dose of ionizing radiation (> ≈ 0.1 Gy) over a short period of time (> ≈ 0.1 Gy/h). Alpha and beta radiation have low penetrating power and are unlikely to affect vital internal organs from outside the body. Any type of ionizing radiation can cause burns, but alpha and beta radiation can only do so if [radioactive contamination](/wiki/Radioactive_contamination "Radioactive contamination") or [nuclear fallout](/wiki/Nuclear_fallout "Nuclear fallout") is deposited on the individual's skin or clothing.

Gamma and neutron radiation can travel much greater distances and penetrate the body easily, so whole-body irradiation generally causes ARS before skin effects are evident. Local gamma irradiation can cause skin effects without any sickness. In the early twentieth century, radiographers would commonly calibrate their machines by irradiating their own hands and measuring the time to onset of [erythema](/wiki/Erythema "Erythema").[[26]](#cite_note-26)

### Accidental

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_radiation_syndrome&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Accidental")]

Main article: [Nuclear and radiation accidents and incidents](/wiki/Nuclear_and_radiation_accidents_and_incidents "Nuclear and radiation accidents and incidents")

Accidental exposure may be the result of a [criticality](/wiki/Criticality_accident "Criticality accident") or [radiotherapy](/wiki/Radiotherapy "Radiotherapy") accident. There have been [numerous](#Notable_incidents) criticality accidents dating back to [atomic testing](/wiki/Manhattan_project "Manhattan project") during World War II, while computer-controlled radiation therapy machines such as [Therac-25](/wiki/Therac-25 "Therac-25") played a major part in radiotherapy accidents. The latter of the two is caused by the failure of equipment software used to monitor the radiational dose given. Human error has played a large part in accidental exposure incidents, including some of the criticality accidents, and larger scale events such as the [Chernobyl disaster](/wiki/Chernobyl_disaster "Chernobyl disaster"). Other events have to do with [orphan sources](/wiki/Orphan_source "Orphan source"), in which radioactive material is unknowingly kept, sold, or stolen. The [Goiânia accident](/wiki/Goi%C3%A2nia_accident "Goiânia accident") is an example, where a forgotten radioactive source was taken from a hospital, resulting in the deaths of 4 people from ARS.[[27]](#cite_note-MTCDPub-27) [Theft and attempted theft](/wiki/Crimes_involving_radioactive_substances#Intentional_or_attempted_theft_of_radioactive_material "Crimes involving radioactive substances") of radioactive material by clueless thieves has also led to lethal exposure in at least one incident.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Exposure may also come from routine spaceflight and [solar flares](/wiki/Solar_flare "Solar flare") that result in radiation effects on earth in the form of [solar storms](/wiki/Solar_storm "Solar storm"). During spaceflight, astronauts are exposed to both [galactic cosmic radiation](/wiki/Galactic_cosmic_ray "Galactic cosmic ray") (GCR) and [solar particle event](/wiki/Solar_proton_event "Solar proton event") (SPE) radiation. The exposure particularly occurs during flights beyond [low Earth orbit](/wiki/Low_Earth_orbit "Low Earth orbit") (LEO). Evidence indicates past SPE radiation levels that would have been lethal for unprotected astronauts.[[29]](#cite_note-29) GCR levels that might lead to acute radiation poisoning are less well understood.[[30]](#cite_note-30) The latter cause is rarer, with an event possibly occurring during the [solar storm of 1859](/wiki/Solar_storm_of_1859 "Solar storm of 1859").

Intentional exposure is controversial as it involves the use of [nuclear weapons](/wiki/Nuclear_weapon "Nuclear weapon"), [human experiments](/wiki/Human_radiation_experiments "Human radiation experiments"), or is given to a victim in an act of murder. The intentional atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki resulted in tens of thousands of casualties; the survivors of these bombings are known today as *[hibakusha](/wiki/Hibakusha "Hibakusha")*. Nuclear weapons emit large amounts of [thermal radiation](/wiki/Thermal_radiation "Thermal radiation") as visible, infrared, and ultraviolet light, to which the atmosphere is largely transparent. This event is also known as "flash", where radiant heat and light [are bombarded](/wiki/Effects_of_nuclear_explosions#Survivability "Effects of nuclear explosions") into any given victim's exposed skin, causing radiation burns.[[31]](#cite_note-Nuclear_Bomb_Effects-31) Death is highly likely, and radiation poisoning is almost certain if one is caught in the open with no terrain or building masking-effects within a radius of 0–3 km from a 1 megaton airburst. The [50% chance of death](/wiki/LD50 "LD50") from the blast extends out to ≈ 8 km from a 1 megaton atmospheric explosion.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Scientific testing on humans within the United States occurred extensively throughout the atomic age. Experiments took place on a range of subjects including, but not limited to; the disabled, children, soldiers, and incarcerated persons, with the level of understanding and consent given by subjects varying from complete to none. Since 1997 there have been requirements for patients to give informed consent, and to be notified if experiments were classified.[[33]](#cite_note-AH-33) The [Soviet nuclear program](/wiki/Soviet_nuclear_program "Soviet nuclear program") also included medical study of exposed citizens living near [Semipalatinsk Test Site](/wiki/Semipalatinsk_Test_Site "Semipalatinsk Test Site"), withholding information about nuclear testing or health risks.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35) The program also involved severe radiation exposures to workers of the [Mayak](/wiki/Mayak "Mayak") facility, especially the early work of [forced laborers](/wiki/Forced_labor_in_the_Soviet_Union "Forced labor in the Soviet Union") on the [A-1 nuclear reactor](/wiki/A-1_(nuclear_reactor) "A-1 (nuclear reactor)"). Criminal activity has involved murder and attempted murder carried out through abrupt victim contact with a radioactive substance such as [polonium](/wiki/Polonium "Polonium") or [plutonium](/wiki/Plutonium "Plutonium"). The death of [Alexander Litvinenko](/wiki/Alexander_Litvinenko "Alexander Litvinenko") is widely believed to be [assassination via polonium poisoning](/wiki/Radiological_warfare#Radiological_assassination "Radiological warfare") and on the order of the Russian [FSB](/wiki/Federal_Security_Service "Federal Security Service"),[[36]](#cite_note-death-36) and polonium is also suspected as the cause of death for [Yasser Arafat](/wiki/Cause_of_Yasser_Arafat%27s_death#Poisoning_with_polonium "Cause of Yasser Arafat's death"),[[37]](#cite_note-37) [Yuri Shchekochikhin](/wiki/Yuri_Shchekochikhin "Yuri Shchekochikhin"), [Lecha Islamov](/w/index.php?title=Lecha_Islamov&action=edit&redlink=1 "Lecha Islamov (page does not exist)") and [Roman Tsepov](/wiki/Roman_Tsepov "Roman Tsepov").[[38]](#cite_note-Sweeney-38)

## Pathophysiology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_radiation_syndrome&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Pathophysiology")]

The most commonly used predictor of ARS is the whole-body [absorbed dose](/wiki/Absorbed_dose "Absorbed dose"). Several related quantities, such as the [equivalent dose](/wiki/Equivalent_dose "Equivalent dose"), [effective dose](/wiki/Effective_dose_(radiation) "Effective dose (radiation)"), and [committed dose](/wiki/Committed_dose "Committed dose"), are used to gauge long-term stochastic biological effects such as cancer incidence, but they are not designed to evaluate ARS.[[39]](#cite_note-ICRP103-39) To help avoid confusion between these quantities, absorbed dose is measured in units of [grays](/wiki/Gray_(unit) "Gray (unit)") (in [SI](/wiki/SI "SI"), unit symbol *Gy*) or [rad](/wiki/Rad_(unit) "Rad (unit)") (in [CGS](/wiki/CGS "CGS")), while the others are measured in [sieverts](/wiki/Sievert_(unit) "Sievert (unit)") (in SI, unit symbol *Sv*) or [rem](/wiki/Rem_(unit) "Rem (unit)") (in CGS). 1 rad = 0.01 Gy and 1 rem = 0.01 Sv.[[40]](#cite_note-remdefinition-40)

In most of the acute exposure scenarios that lead to radiation sickness, the bulk of the radiation is external whole-body gamma, in which case the absorbed, equivalent, and effective doses are all equal. There are exceptions, such as the Therac-25 accidents and the 1958 [Cecil Kelley criticality accident](/wiki/Cecil_Kelley_criticality_accident "Cecil Kelley criticality accident"), where the absorbed doses in Gy or rad are the only useful quantities, because of the targeted nature of the exposure to the body.

[Radiotherapy](/wiki/Radiotherapy "Radiotherapy") treatments are typically prescribed in terms of the local absorbed dose, which might be 60 Gy or higher. The dose is fractionated to about 2 Gy per day for *curative* treatment, which allows normal tissues to undergo [repair](/wiki/DNA_repair "DNA repair"), allowing them to tolerate a higher dose than would otherwise be expected. The dose to the targeted tissue mass must be averaged over the entire body mass, most of which receives negligible radiation, to arrive at a whole-body absorbed dose that can be compared to the table above.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### DNA damage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_radiation_syndrome&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: DNA damage")]

Exposure to high doses of radiation causes [DNA](/wiki/DNA "DNA") damage, later creating serious and even lethal [chromosomal aberrations](/wiki/Chromosome_abnormality "Chromosome abnormality") if left unrepaired. Ionizing radiation can produce [reactive oxygen species](/wiki/Reactive_oxygen_species "Reactive oxygen species"), and does directly damage cells by causing localized ionization events. The former is very damaging to DNA, while the latter events create clusters of DNA damage.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-Eccles_L._2011-42) This damage includes loss of [nucleobases](/wiki/Nucleobases "Nucleobases") and breakage of the sugar-phosphate backbone that binds to the nucleobases. The DNA organization at the level of [histones](/wiki/Histones "Histones"), [nucleosomes](/wiki/Nucleosomes "Nucleosomes"), and [chromatin](/wiki/Chromatin "Chromatin") also affects its susceptibility to [radiation damage](/wiki/Radiation_damage "Radiation damage").[[43]](#cite_note-43) Clustered damage, defined as at least two lesions within a helical turn, is especially harmful.[[42]](#cite_note-Eccles_L._2011-42) While DNA damage happens frequently and naturally in the cell from endogenous sources, clustered damage is a unique effect of radiation exposure.[[44]](#cite_note-44) Clustered damage takes longer to repair than isolated breakages, and is less likely to be repaired at all.[[45]](#cite_note-45) Larger radiation doses are more prone to cause tighter clustering of damage, and closely localized damage is increasingly less likely to be repaired.[[42]](#cite_note-Eccles_L._2011-42)

Somatic mutations cannot be passed down from parent to offspring, but these mutations can propagate in cell lines within an organism. Radiation damage can also cause chromosome and [chromatid](/wiki/Chromatid "Chromatid") aberrations, and their effects depend on in which stage of the mitotic cycle the cell is when the irradiation occurs. If the cell is in [interphase](/wiki/Interphase "Interphase"), while it is still a single strand of chromatin, the damage will be replicated during the S1 phase of the [cell cycle](/wiki/Cell_cycle "Cell cycle"), and there will be a break on both chromosome arms; the damage then will be apparent in both [daughter cells](/wiki/Daughter_cells "Daughter cells"). If the irradiation occurs after replication, only one arm will bear the damage; this damage will be apparent in only one daughter cell. A damaged chromosome may cyclize, binding to another chromosome, or to itself.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

## Diagnosis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_radiation_syndrome&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Diagnosis")]

Diagnosis is typically made based on a history of significant radiation exposure and suitable clinical findings.[[3]](#cite_note-SMJ2010-3) An [absolute lymphocyte count](/wiki/Complete_blood_count "Complete blood count") can give a rough estimate of radiation exposure.[[3]](#cite_note-SMJ2010-3) Time from exposure to vomiting can also give estimates of exposure levels if they are less than 10 Gy (1000 rad).[[3]](#cite_note-SMJ2010-3)

## Prevention

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_radiation_syndrome&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Prevention")]

A guiding principle of radiation safety is *as low as reasonably achievable* (ALARA).[[47]](#cite_note-CDC2015Safe-47) This means try to avoid exposure as much as possible and includes the three components of time, distance, and shielding.[[47]](#cite_note-CDC2015Safe-47)

### Time

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_radiation_syndrome&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Time")]

The longer that humans are subjected to radiation the larger the dose will be. The advice in the [nuclear war](/wiki/Nuclear_warfare "Nuclear warfare") manual entitled *[Nuclear War Survival Skills](/wiki/Nuclear_War_Survival_Skills "Nuclear War Survival Skills")* published by [Cresson Kearny](/wiki/Cresson_Kearny "Cresson Kearny") in the [U.S.](/wiki/United_States "United States") was that if one needed to leave the shelter then this should be done as rapidly as possible to minimize exposure.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

In chapter 12, he states that "[q]uickly putting or dumping wastes outside is not hazardous once fallout is no longer being deposited. For example, assume the shelter is in an area of heavy fallout and the dose rate outside is 400 [roentgen (R)](/wiki/Roentgen_(unit) "Roentgen (unit)") per hour, enough to give a potentially fatal dose in about an hour to a person exposed in the open. If a person needs to be exposed for only 10 seconds to dump a bucket, in this 1/360 of an hour he will receive a dose of only about 1 R. Under war conditions, an additional 1-R dose is of little concern." In peacetime, radiation workers are taught to work as quickly as possible when performing a task that exposes them to radiation, such as for instance during recovery of a radioactive source.

### Shielding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_radiation_syndrome&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Shielding")]

See also: [Radiation protection](/wiki/Radiation_protection "Radiation protection")

Usually, matter attenuates radiation, so placing any mass (e.g., lead, dirt, sandbags, vehicles, water, even air) between humans and the source will reduce the radiation dose. This is not always the case, however; care should be taken when constructing shielding for a specific purpose. For example, although high atomic number materials are very effective in shielding [photons](/wiki/Photon "Photon"), using them to shield [beta particles](/wiki/Beta_particle "Beta particle") may cause higher radiation exposure due to the production of [bremsstrahlung](/wiki/Bremsstrahlung "Bremsstrahlung") x-rays, and hence low atomic number materials are recommended. Also, using material with a high [neutron activation](/wiki/Neutron_activation "Neutron activation") [cross section](/wiki/Cross_section_(physics) "Cross section (physics)") to shield neutrons will result in the shielding material itself becoming radioactive and hence more dangerous than if it were not present.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

There are many types of shielding strategies that can be used to reduce the effects of radiation exposure. Internal contamination protective equipment such as respirators are used to prevent internal deposition as a result of inhalation and ingestion of radioactive material. Dermal protective equipment, which protects against external contamination, provides shielding to prevent radioactive material from being deposited on external structures.[[49]](#cite_note-49) While these protective measures do provide a barrier from radioactive material deposition, they do not shield from externally penetrating gamma radiation. This leaves anyone exposed to penetrating gamma rays at high risk of ARS.

Naturally, shielding the entire body from high energy gamma radiation is optimal, but the required mass to provide adequate attenuation makes functional movement nearly impossible. In the event of a radiation catastrophe, medical and security personnel need [mobile protection equipment](/wiki/Radiation_protection#External_penetrating_radiation "Radiation protection") in order to safely assist in containment, evacuation, and many other necessary public safety objectives.

Research has been done exploring the feasibility of partial body shielding, a radiation protection strategy that provides adequate attenuation to only the most radio-sensitive organs and tissues inside the body. Irreversible stem cell damage in the bone marrow is the first life-threatening effect of intense radiation exposure and therefore one of the most important bodily elements to protect. Due to the regenerative property of [hematopoietic stem cells](/wiki/Hematopoietic_stem_cell "Hematopoietic stem cell"), it is only necessary to protect enough bone marrow to repopulate the exposed areas of the body with the shielded supply.[[50]](#cite_note-50) This concept allows for the development of lightweight mobile radiation protection equipment, which provides adequate protection, deferring the onset of ARS to much higher exposure doses. One example of such equipment is the [360 Gamma](/wiki/StemRad#360_Gamma "StemRad"), a radiation protection belt that applies selective shielding to protect the bone marrow stored in the pelvic area as well as other radio sensitive organs in the abdominal region without hindering functional mobility.

### Reduction of incorporation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_radiation_syndrome&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Reduction of incorporation")]

Where radioactive contamination is present, an [elastomeric respirator](/wiki/Elastomeric_respirator "Elastomeric respirator"), [dust mask](/wiki/Dust_mask "Dust mask"), or good hygiene practices may offer protection, depending on the nature of the contaminant. [Potassium iodide](/wiki/Potassium_iodide "Potassium iodide") (KI) tablets can reduce the risk of cancer in some situations due to slower uptake of ambient radioiodine. Although this does not protect any organ other than the thyroid gland, their effectiveness is still highly dependent on the time of ingestion, which would protect the gland for the duration of a twenty-four-hour period. They do not prevent ARS as they provide no shielding from other environmental radionuclides.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

### Fractionation of dose

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_radiation_syndrome&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Fractionation of dose")]

Main article: [Dose fractionation](/wiki/Dose_fractionation "Dose fractionation")

If an intentional dose is broken up into a number of smaller doses, with time allowed for recovery between irradiations, the same total dose causes less [cell death](/wiki/Cell_death "Cell death"). Even without interruptions, a reduction in dose rate below 0.1 Gy/h also tends to reduce cell death.[[39]](#cite_note-ICRP103-39) This technique is routinely used in radiotherapy

The human body contains many types of [cells](/wiki/Cell_(biology) "Cell (biology)") and a human can be killed by the loss of a single type of cells in a vital organ. For many short term radiation deaths (3–30 days), the loss of two important types of cells that are constantly being regenerated causes death. The loss of cells forming [blood cells](/wiki/Blood_cells "Blood cells") ([bone marrow](/wiki/Bone_marrow "Bone marrow")) and the cells in the digestive system ([microvilli](/wiki/Intestinal_villus "Intestinal villus"), which form part of the wall of the [intestines](/wiki/Intestines "Intestines")) is fatal.

## Management

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_radiation_syndrome&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Management")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Death_by_haematopoietic_syndrome_of_radiation_sickness-_influence_of_medical_care.png/250px-Death_by_haematopoietic_syndrome_of_radiation_sickness-_influence_of_medical_care.png)](/wiki/File:Death_by_haematopoietic_syndrome_of_radiation_sickness-_influence_of_medical_care.png)

Effect of medical care on acute radiation syndrome

Treatment usually involves supportive care with possible symptomatic measures employed. The former involves the possible use of [antibiotics](/wiki/Antibiotics "Antibiotics"), [blood products](/wiki/Blood_products "Blood products"), [colony stimulating factors](/wiki/Colony_stimulating_factors "Colony stimulating factors"), and [stem cell transplant](/wiki/Stem_cell_transplant "Stem cell transplant").[[3]](#cite_note-SMJ2010-3)

### Antimicrobials

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_radiation_syndrome&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Antimicrobials")]

Main article: [Treatment of infections after exposure to ionizing radiation](/wiki/Treatment_of_infections_after_exposure_to_ionizing_radiation "Treatment of infections after exposure to ionizing radiation")

There is a direct relationship between the degree of the [neutropenia](/wiki/Neutropenia "Neutropenia") that emerges after exposure to radiation and the increased risk of developing infection. Since there are no controlled studies of therapeutic intervention in humans, most of the current recommendations are based on animal research.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [treatment](/wiki/Treatment_of_infections_after_accidental_or_hostile_exposure_to_ionizing_radiation "Treatment of infections after accidental or hostile exposure to ionizing radiation") of established or suspected infection following exposure to radiation (characterized by neutropenia and fever) is similar to the one used for other febrile neutropenic patients. However, important differences between the two conditions exist. Individuals that develop neutropenia after exposure to radiation are also susceptible to irradiation damage in other tissues, such as the gastrointestinal tract, lungs and central nervous system. These patients may require therapeutic interventions not needed in other types of neutropenic patients. The response of irradiated animals to antimicrobial therapy can be unpredictable, as was evident in experimental studies where [metronidazole](/wiki/Metronidazole "Metronidazole")[[52]](#cite_note-52) and [pefloxacin](/wiki/Pefloxacin "Pefloxacin")[[53]](#cite_note-53) therapies were detrimental.

Antimicrobials that reduce the number of the strict [anaerobic](/wiki/Anaerobic_infection "Anaerobic infection") component of the gut flora (i.e., metronidazole) generally should not be given because they may enhance systemic infection by aerobic or [facultative bacteria](/wiki/Facultative_bacteria "Facultative bacteria"), thus facilitating mortality after irradiation.[[54]](#cite_note-54)

An empirical regimen of antimicrobials should be chosen based on the pattern of bacterial susceptibility and [nosocomial infections](/wiki/Nosocomial_infections "Nosocomial infections") in the affected area and medical center and the degree of neutropenia. Broad-spectrum empirical therapy (see below for choices) with high doses of one or more antibiotics should be initiated at the onset of fever. These antimicrobials should be directed at the eradication of Gram-negative aerobic bacilli (i.e., [Enterobacteriaceae](/wiki/Enterobacteriaceae "Enterobacteriaceae"), *[Pseudomonas](/wiki/Pseudomonas "Pseudomonas")*) that account for more than three quarters of the isolates causing sepsis. Because aerobic and facultative Gram-positive bacteria (mostly [alpha-hemolytic streptococci](/wiki/Alpha-hemolytic_streptococci "Alpha-hemolytic streptococci")) cause [sepsis](/wiki/Sepsis "Sepsis") in about a quarter of the victims, coverage for these organisms may also be needed.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

A standardized management plan for people with neutropenia and fever should be devised. Empirical regimens contain antibiotics broadly active against Gram-negative aerobic bacteria ([quinolones](/wiki/Quinolone_antibiotic "Quinolone antibiotic"): i.e., [ciprofloxacin](/wiki/Ciprofloxacin "Ciprofloxacin"), [levofloxacin](/wiki/Levofloxacin "Levofloxacin"), a third- or fourth-generation cephalosporin with pseudomonal coverage: e.g., [cefepime](/wiki/Cefepime "Cefepime"), [ceftazidime](/wiki/Ceftazidime "Ceftazidime"), or an aminoglycoside: i.e. [gentamicin](/wiki/Gentamicin "Gentamicin"), [amikacin](/wiki/Amikacin "Amikacin")).[[56]](#cite_note-56)

## Prognosis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_radiation_syndrome&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Prognosis")]

See also: [Radiation-induced cancer](/wiki/Radiation-induced_cancer "Radiation-induced cancer")

The prognosis for ARS is dependent on the exposure dose, with anything above 8 Gy being almost always lethal, even with medical care.[[4]](#cite_note-NORD2019-4)[[57]](#cite_note-57) [Radiation burns](#Skin_changes) from lower-level exposures usually manifest after 2 months, while reactions from the burns occur months to years after radiation treatment.[[58]](#cite_note-Andrews-58)[[59]](#cite_note-pmid10580952-59) Complications from ARS include an increased risk of developing radiation-induced cancer later in life. According to the controversial but commonly applied [linear no-threshold model](/wiki/Linear_no-threshold_model "Linear no-threshold model"), any exposure to ionizing radiation, even at doses too low to produce any symptoms of radiation sickness, can induce cancer due to cellular and genetic damage. The probability of developing cancer is a linear function with respect to the [effective radiation dose](/wiki/Effective_radiation_dose "Effective radiation dose"). Radiation cancer may occur after ionizing radiation exposure following a latent period averaging 20 to 40 years.[[60]](#cite_note-pmid15486177-60)[[58]](#cite_note-Andrews-58)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_radiation_syndrome&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: History")]

Acute effects of ionizing radiation were first observed when [Wilhelm Röntgen](/wiki/Wilhelm_R%C3%B6ntgen "Wilhelm Röntgen") intentionally subjected his fingers to X-rays in 1895. He published his observations concerning the burns that developed that eventually healed, and misattributed them to ozone. Röntgen believed the [free radical](/wiki/Free_radical "Free radical") produced in air by X-rays from the ozone was the cause, but other free radicals produced within the body are now understood to be more important. David Walsh first established the symptoms of radiation sickness in 1897.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

Ingestion of radioactive materials caused many [radiation-induced cancers](/wiki/Radiation-induced_cancer "Radiation-induced cancer") in the 1930s, but no one was exposed to high enough doses at high enough rates to bring on ARS.

The [atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki](/wiki/Atomic_bombings_of_Hiroshima_and_Nagasaki "Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki") resulted in high acute doses of radiation to a large number of Japanese people, allowing for greater insight into its symptoms and dangers. Red Cross Hospital Surgeon [Terufumi Sasaki](/wiki/Terufumi_Sasaki "Terufumi Sasaki") led intensive research into the syndrome in the weeks and months following the Hiroshima and Nagasaki bombings. Sasaki and his team were able to monitor the effects of radiation in patients of varying proximities to the blast itself, leading to the establishment of three recorded stages of the syndrome. Within 25–30 days of the explosion, Sasaki noticed a sharp drop in white blood cell count and established this drop, along with symptoms of fever, as prognostic standards for ARS.[[62]](#cite_note-62) Actress [Midori Naka](/wiki/Midori_Naka "Midori Naka"), who was present during the atomic bombing of Hiroshima, was the first incident of radiation poisoning to be extensively studied. Her death on 24 August 1945 was the first death ever to be officially certified as a result of ARS (or "Atomic bomb disease").

There are two major databases that track radiation accidents: The American [ORISE](/wiki/ORISE "ORISE") REAC/TS and the European [IRSN](/wiki/IRSN "IRSN") ACCIRAD. REAC/TS shows 417 accidents occurring between 1944 and 2000, causing about 3000 cases of ARS, of which 127 were fatal.[[63]](#cite_note-63) ACCIRAD lists 580 accidents with 180 ARS fatalities for an almost identical period.[[64]](#cite_note-64) The two deliberate bombings are not included in either database, nor are any possible radiation-induced cancers from low doses. The detailed accounting is difficult because of confounding factors. ARS may be accompanied by conventional injuries such as steam burns, or may occur in someone with a pre-existing condition undergoing radiotherapy. There may be multiple causes for death, and the contribution from radiation may be unclear. Some documents may incorrectly refer to radiation-induced cancers as radiation poisoning, or may count all overexposed individuals as survivors without mentioning if they had any symptoms of ARS.

### Notable cases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_radiation_syndrome&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Notable cases")]

The following table includes only individuals known to have symptoms of ARS. Excluded are cases of chronic radiation syndrome, such as the case of [Albert Stevens](/wiki/Albert_Stevens "Albert Stevens"). Chronic radiation syndrome is characterized by an exposure of lower doses of radiation, over a longer duration, than with ARS. The table also excludes cases where an individual was exposed to so much radiation that death occurred before medical assistance or dose estimations could be made, such as an attempted cobalt-60 thief who reportedly died 30 minutes after exposure.[[65]](#cite_note-65) The result column represents the time from exposure to the time of death. As ARS is measured by a whole-body [absorbed dose](/wiki/Absorbed_dose "Absorbed dose"), the exposure column only includes units of gray (Gy).

## Other animals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Acute_radiation_syndrome&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Other animals")]

Thousands of scientific experiments have been performed to study ARS in animals.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] There is a simple guide for predicting survival and death in mammals, including humans, following the acute effects of inhaling radioactive particles.[[73]](#cite_note-73)
